- [Section 2 (Chapters 8 and 9): Purkait, N. (2019), Neural Networks with Keras, Packt.](https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/1239038)

From <[https://app.qa.com/resource/topic-7-machine-learning-using-cloud-computing-ai-1698/?context_id=15082&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/resource/topic-7-machine-learning-using-cloud-computing-ai-1698/?context_id=15082&context_resource=lp)>

# Book

[Section 2 (Chapters 8 and 9): Purkait, N. (2019), Neural Networks with Keras, Packt.](https://www.vlebooks.com/Product/Index/1239038)

## Autoencoders

Autoencoders used to be used in in initialising layer for deep CNN known as greedy layer wise pretraining. But better random weight initialisation ended up being better.

Single layer autoencoders with a linear activation function are similar to Principal component analysis(PCA)

AE have the benefit of non-linear encoders and decoder functions whereas PCA algorithms is restricted to a linear map.

It's the representation which is often the useful bit.

Contractive autoencoders(CAE)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

So even if the input is slightly different it can perform well.

To use it you can make two mirrors of the encoding and decoding parts

For images using CNN, with the dimensions going down using pooling, is how you make a AE with that.

To denoise the autoencoder, you make a noisy input, but original output.

### Generative Networks

With generative models, they try and answer how likely are these features(x) present in an instance of data of class y.

It's different than for supervised learning which is how likely is this instance party of class, y given features x are present.

You might investigate a latent space, and find say a smile vector or a gender vector. Which could then be modified to create new content.

Use DNN to learn probability distributions of variables over a reduced latent space.

Want some randomness so outputs are more creative.

One method to add some randomness to the input, e.g. z sampled from a gaussian distribution

So f(x,z) not simply f(x)

When sampling from the laten space, it's random.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Since it's not a function it's hard to backpropagate.

So create a transformation on a random variable

![](data:image/png;base64,)

In larger networks these can be estimated by parts of the larger NN.

 μ = f(x ;θ) and σ = g(x; θ), where the term θ denotes the learnable parameters of a neural network

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Then can differentiate a loss function J(y) for backpropgation.

Differentiable generator function g (l ; θ(g) ) which transforms samples from a continuous latent space(l) to generate an output. This function itself is what's being approximated by the neural network.

Variational Autoencoders(VAE)

Generative Adversarial networks(GAN)

VAE are good at learning from well-structured space, but suffer from blurry reconstructions.

GANs do better at generating realistic content, despite sampling from unstructured and discontinuous latent space

#### VAE

Uses a continuous latent space. If you sample from a discrete one outputs could be unrealistic.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

When connecting 256 to 2 dim, there are 2 bias terms so 514 params

z = z_mean + exp(z_log_variance) * epsilon

Epsilon is a small tensor

There are two loss function

- Reconstruction loss which ensures the decoded output matches the supplied inputs
- Regularisation loss, which stops the model from overfitting

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Generating by iterating through the latent space. The latent space is only 2 dim, which is interesting.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The sampling and loss, are what make the z_mean and z_log_var to be what they are, otherwise it would blow up.

### GAN

![](data:image/png;base64,)

GAN are difficult to train, more like an art than science.

GAN use leakyRelu and tanh instead of Rectified Lunear Unit(ReLU) and signoid activation functions

If using tanh, normalise between -1 and 1

Deep Convolutional Generative Adversarial Network(DCGAN)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Gradient sparsity is normally a good thing, but not for GANs, where leakyReLU is better.

Max pooling isn't used either.

You compile the discriminator and the overall model so you don't need to directly compile the generator.

Loss during training isn't smooth

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Generators can make good quality images, which is impressive since it never sees real images, it's all through feedback from the discriminator.

# Main

## Autoencoders and generative networks

Autoencoders are a NN that encodes complex input onto a simpler latent representation called a latent space.

Latent space is usually smaller than the original data but not always.

Use of autoencoders is used in generative networks. The autoencoder has learnt some latent representation of input data and the latent space can be sampled to generate new previously unseen objects.

An auto encoder is an ANN used to learn efficient representations of un-labelled data. They have two functions

1. Encoding that transforms the input data
2. Decoding that recreates the input data from the encoded representation.

Originally used for dimensionality reduction or feature learning. Can reduce noise.

Input data can be various types such as  images, natural language or time series data

Can be used for facial recognition, feature detection, anomaly detection and meaning of words

![This image illustrates the structure of an autoencoder, a type of neural network used for unsupervised learning. On the left side, there are blue circles representing the input data. The input data is processed by the encoder, which compresses it into a lower-dimensional latent space, depicted by the red circles in the middle. This compressed representation is then passed through the decoder on the right, which reconstructs the data into its original form, shown by the yellow circles. Arrows indicate the flow of data from input to encoded data and then to reconstructed data, highlighting the two main parts of the autoencoder: the encoder and the decoder.](data:image/png;base64,)

It learns from its own inputs so is self-supervised

![An example autoencoder as described in the text. The image illustrates a neural network architecture known as an autoencoder. It features an encoder on the left, which includes input data represented by blue squares, and two hidden layers (green and purple) that progressively reduce the data's dimensionality. In the middle, a red layer labelled Code represents the compressed data. On the right, the decoder section, which mirrors the encoder, reconstructs the data through two hidden layers (purple and green) to the output, shown as blue squares. Dashed lines depict the connections between neurons across all layers.](data:image/png;base64,)

Learning mechanism is

min L(x, g(f(x))

Where f(x) is encoding and g(h) is decoding

Autoencoders are data specific. They will only work well with data like the training data.

They compress objects, but don't perform better than standard image compression algorithms like JPEG

The benefit is the latest representation which captures meaning of the input data, that other compression techniques don't do.

They are lossy.

### Types and topologies

Undercomplete autoencoder where the latent space is smaller than the input.

Overcomplete autoencoder has higher dimensional latent space compared to input layer. So has additional encoding capacity. Used for denoising.

Shallow autoencoder has just one hidden layer of neurons

Deep autoencoder has many hidden layers. If there are too many layers it might learn too much and might be less creative at handling or generating new data.

Sparse autoencoder. Good for dim reduction or feature extraction.

Variational autoencoder. Good for gen AI.

Denoising autoencoder. Work by adding noise to good images and then learn to remove them.

![This image showcases four different types of autoencoder architectures. The top-left quadrant displays a "Shallow Undercomplete" autoencoder with fewer hidden units than input units, represented by a simple network structure. The top-right quadrant illustrates a "Shallow Overcomplete" autoencoder with more hidden units than input units, resulting in an expanded middle layer. The bottom-left quadrant shows a "Deep Undercomplete" autoencoder, featuring multiple layers and fewer hidden units compared to input units. The bottom-right quadrant presents a "Deep Overcomplete" autoencoder with multiple layers and more hidden units than input units. Each quadrant includes networks with blue circles for input and output units, red circles for hidden units, and green circles indicating the varied complexity and capacity of the autoencoders.](data:image/png;base64,)

![The image shows four different types of autoencoders: Auto Encoder (AE), Sparse Auto Encoder (SAE), Variational Auto Encoder (VAE), and Denoising Auto Encoder (DAE). Each type is represented with its unique neural network structure. AE consists of yellow input cells, green hidden cells, and orange match input-output cells. SAE has a similar structure but with fewer active hidden cells, indicated by green circles. VAE includes probabilistic hidden cells shown with green circles outlined in black. DAE incorporates noisy input cells, marked as yellow triangles. The connections between neurons are depicted with black lines, illustrating the data flow within each network.](data:image/png;base64,)
